title: Getting started with daos
text: nowf() returns current time formatted string. Useful building timestamped file names. Combine two quick timestamped path:
code: nowf()                  # default: YYYYMMDD #> [1] "20260519" nowf("%Y-%m-%d %H:%M")  # custom format #> [1] "2026-05-19 02:10" f("log/{nowf()}/pipeline.log")

code: quiet(message("this will not appear")) quiet(warning("neither will this"))  # Useful when loading packages without the startup banner: # quiet(library(tidyverse))

title: Getting started with daos
text: Unlike rlang::is_empty(), is_blank() treats empty strings -NA vectors blank.
code: is_blank(NULL)        # TRUE #> [1] TRUE is_blank(NA)          # TRUE #> [1] TRUE is_blank("")          # TRUE #> [1] TRUE is_blank(c(NA, NA))   # TRUE #> [1] TRUE is_blank(0)           # FALSE #> [1] FALSE is_blank("text")      # FALSE #> [1] FALSE

title: Getting started with daos
text: Returns right-hand side whenever left-hand side blank (using logic is_blank()).
code: NULL %??% "default" #> [1] "default" NA   %??% 0 #> [1] 0 ""   %??% "unknown" #> [1] "unknown" 42   %??% 99          # returns 42 #> [1] 42

title: Getting started with daos
text: require_files() expands glue syntax path strings aborts immediately clear message file missing. returns named character vector ready pipe read_files().
code: # Expand {0:9} into ten paths and verify they all exist: require_files("data/dat{0:9}.parquet")

title: Getting started with daos
text: read_files() detects format extension dispatches right reader. single path returns object directly; multiple paths return named list progress bar. Supported formats: csv, tsv, parquet, feather, xlsx, xls, rds, sas7bdat, sav, por, xpt, dta, json, ndjson, jsonl, yaml, yml, txt.
code: # Single file: df <- read_files("data/results.parquet")  # Multiple files — returns a named list: files <- read_files(c("data/a.csv", "data/b.csv"))

what: bind_files() — row-bind a list of data frames
title: Getting started with daos
text: reading multiple files list, bind_files() combines row-wise. source column (whatever .id names ) records row came . types differ, set .guess = TRUE auto-convert:
code: df1 <- data.frame(year = 2020L, value = 10.5) df2 <- data.frame(year = 2021L, value = 11.2) bind_files(list(a = df1, b = df2)) #>   source year value #> 1      a 2020  10.5 #> 2      b 2021  11.2 bind_files(list_of_files, .guess = TRUE)

code: require_files("data/dat{0:9}.parquet") |>   read_files() |>   bind_files()

what: unpack_files() — assign list elements as variables
title: Getting started with daos

code: require_files("data/dat{0:9}.parquet") |>   read_files() |>   unpack_files() # dat0, dat1, ..., dat9 are now in your environment

what: summon() — retrieve objects by name pattern
title: Getting started with daos
text: Collect objects whose names match regex back list:
code: dat1 <- data.frame(x = 1) dat2 <- data.frame(x = 2) dat3 <- data.frame(x = 3)  result <- summon("^dat\\d+$") names(result) #> [1] "dat1" "dat2" "dat3"

what: view_types() — compare column types across data frames
title: Getting started with daos
text: view_types() shows pillar::type_sum() type column every dataset supplied. Invaluable joins calling bind_files().
code: df_a <- data.frame(x = 1L,  y = "a", z = TRUE) df_b <- data.frame(x = 1.0, y = "b", z = 1L)  # All columns: view_types(df_a, df_b) #> # A tibble: 3 × 3 #>   column df_a  df_b  #>   <chr>  <chr> <chr> #> 1 x      int   dbl   #> 2 y      chr   chr   #> 3 z      lgl   int # Only columns where types differ: view_types(df_a, df_b, diff = TRUE) #> # A tibble: 2 × 3 #>   column df_a  df_b  #>   <chr>  <chr> <chr> #> 1 x      int   dbl   #> 2 z      lgl   int # Check that a specific column has the expected type: view_types(df_a, df_b, focus = c(x = "int")) #> # A tibble: 1 × 2 #>   column df_b  #>   <chr>  <chr> #> 1 x      dbl # Returns 0 rows if all match, otherwise shows the offending datasets

what: size_env() — object sizes in an environment
title: Getting started with daos
code: big   <- 1:1e6 small <- letters size_env()        # all objects, largest first #> # A tibble: 11 × 3 #>    name      size      pretty #>    <chr>    <dbl> <fs::bytes> #>  1 big    4000048       3.81M #>  2 result    2648       2.59K #>  3 small     1712       1.67K #>  4 df_a      1064       1.04K #>  5 df_b      1064       1.04K #>  6 df1        864         864 #>  7 df2        864         864 #>  8 dat1       736         736 #>  9 dat2       736         736 #> 10 dat3       736         736 #> 11 year        56          56 size_env(n = 2)   # top 2 only #> # A tibble: 2 × 3 #>   name      size      pretty #>   <chr>    <dbl> <fs::bytes> #> 1 big    4000048       3.81M #> 2 result    2648       2.59K

what: expect_empty() — assert a data frame is empty
title: Getting started with daos
text: Use expect_empty() checkpoint pipeline. succeeds silently data frame rows, warns rows (unless abort_msg set, case aborts). log argument writes timestamped entry file — useful automated pipelines want minimal audit trail:
code: # Success: data.frame() |> expect_empty(success_msg = "All good — no rows") #> ✔ All good — no rows  # Warning: filter(ggplot2::mpg, cyl < 0) |>   expect_empty(warn_msg = "Unexpected: negative cylinder count") #> ✔ The dataset is empty. # Hard error: filter(dplyr::starwars, height < 0) |>   expect_empty(abort_msg = "Impossible: negative height") log_path <- f("log/{nowf()}/checks.log") checker  <- purrr::partial(expect_empty, log = log_path)  filter(dplyr::starwars, name == "Harry Potter") |>   checker(success_msg = "No fictional characters")  filter(dplyr::starwars, height > 250) |>   checker(warn_msg = "Unusually tall characters found")

ℹ 224 more rows #> # ℹ 2 more variables: fl <chr>, class <chr> # Check specific columns: flag_duplicates(ggplot2::mpg, manufacturer, model, year) |>   filter(isdup) |>   arrange(dupid) #> # A tibble: 227 × 13 #>    isdup dupid manufacturer model      displ  year   cyl trans drv     cty   hwy #>    <lgl> <int> <chr>        <chr>      <dbl> <int> <int> <chr> <chr> <int> <int> #>  1 TRUE      1 audi         a4           1.8  1999     4 auto… f        18    29 #>  2 TRUE      1 audi         a4           1.8  1999     4 manu… f        21    29 #>  3 TRUE      1 audi         a4           2.8  1999     6 auto… f        16    26 #>  4 TRUE      1 audi         a4           2.8  1999     6 manu… f        18    26 #>  5 TRUE      2 audi         a4           2    2008     4 manu… f        20    31 #>  6 TRUE      2 audi         a4           2    2008     4 auto… f        21    30 #>  7 TRUE      2 audi         a4           3.1  2008     6 auto… f        18    27 #>  8 TRUE      3 audi         a4 quattro   1.8  1999     4 manu… 4        18    26 #>  9 TRUE      3 audi         a4 quattro   1.8  1999     4 auto… 4        16    25 #> 10 TRUE      3 audi         a4 quattro   2.8  1999     6 auto… 4        15    25 #> # ℹ 217 more rows #> # ℹ 2 more variables: fl <chr>, class <chr> ggplot2::mpg |>   flag_duplicates() |>   filter(isdup) |>   expect_empty(warn_msg = "Duplicate rows detected") #> Warning: Duplicate rows detected

title: Getting started with daos
text: find_signs() uses meet---middle algorithm find sign assignment (+1, -1, 0) set values signed sum equals specified total. Useful importing accounting data sign conventions unknown. can pin known signs allow max_zeros items excluded:
code: items <- data.frame(   label = c("revenue", "costs", "tax", "total"),   value = c(1000, 400, 100, 500) )  find_signs(items, label, value, total_label = "total") #> # A tibble: 0 × 0 find_signs(   items,   label, value,   total_label = "total",   positive    = "revenue",   negative    = c("costs", "tax"),   max_zeros   = 1L )

title: Getting started with daos
text: time2screen() launches interactive Shiny app reviewing time series group group. columns x, y, series, excluded automatically become dropdown filters. Navigate keyboard (← / →) click buttons. Press Esc exit. Requires shiny highcharter.
code: library(shiny) library(highcharter)  # Simple example — one line per group: df <- expand.grid(   year    = 2010:2020,   country = c("DK", "SE", "NO", "FI") ) df$gdp <- rnorm(nrow(df), mean = 300, sd = 30)  time2screen(df, x = year, y = gdp)  # With a series dimension for multiple lines per group: ggplot2::economics_long |>   time2screen(date, value, series = variable)

title: Row-bind a list of data frames — bind_files
text: Combines list data frames single tibble using purrr::list_rbind(). column types differ across files, informative error raised hints resolution. Setting .guess = TRUE converts columns character first, lets readr::type_convert() infer common type.

code: bind_files(data, .id = "source", .guess = FALSE)

title: Row-bind a list of data frames — bind_files
text: data list data frames tibbles. .id Name source column added identify data frame row originated . Default "source". .guess TRUE, columns coerced character binding re-typed automatically. Use column types differ across files. Default FALSE.

title: Row-bind a list of data frames — bind_files
text: single tibble rows combined source column (whatever .id names ).

title: Row-bind a list of data frames — bind_files
code: df1 <- data.frame(x = 1:3, y = letters[1:3]) df2 <- data.frame(x = 4:5, y = letters[4:5]) bind_files(list(a = df1, b = df2)) #>   source x y #> 1      a 1 a #> 2      a 2 b #> 3      a 3 c #> 4      b 4 d #> 5      b 5 e  # Force type guessing when types differ: df3 <- data.frame(x = c("1", "2")) df4 <- data.frame(x = c(3L, 4L)) bind_files(list(df3, df4), .guess = TRUE) #>  #> ── Column specification ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────── #> cols( #>   x = col_double() #> ) #>   source x #> 1      1 1 #> 2      1 2 #> 3      2 3 #> 4      2 4

what: Extract information from Danish CPR numbers — cpr_info
title: Extract information from Danish CPR numbers — cpr_info
text: Vectorised extraction birth date, age, sex, sequential number, validity indicators Danish CPR (Civil Person Register) numbers. Returns original data frame requested columns appended.

title: Extract information from Danish CPR numbers — cpr_info
code: cpr_info(   data,   cpr_col,   add = c("bday", "age", "sex", "pnum", "mod11", "valid"),   ref_date = Sys.Date() )

title: Extract information from Danish CPR numbers — cpr_info
text: data tibble data frame. cpr_col Name CPR column (unquoted). add info types add. Either: unnamed character vector, e.g. c("bday", "age") — uses type names column names. named character vector, e.g. c(birth_date = "bday", years_old = "age") — left-hand side becomes column name, right-hand side type. Default: six types. ref_date Reference date age calculation. Accepts Date object ISO-format string ("YYYY-MM-DD"). Default: Sys.Date().

title: Extract information from Danish CPR numbers — cpr_info
text: original data frame requested columns appended.

title: Extract information from Danish CPR numbers — cpr_info
text: Supported info types (add values): Century detection follows official CPR Register rules based digit 7 two-digit year component. Input tolerance: dashes spaces stripped automatically. Nine-digit numbers zero-padded left (recovering values lost leading zero Excel).

title: Extract information from Danish CPR numbers — cpr_info
code: df <- data.frame(   pnr = c("1111111118", "111111-1118", "111111118"),   stringsAsFactors = FALSE )  # Default: add all columns cpr_info(df, pnr) #>           pnr       bday age    sex pnum mod11 valid #> 1  1111111118 1911-11-11 114 kvinde 1118  TRUE  TRUE #> 2 111111-1118 1911-11-11 114 kvinde 1118  TRUE  TRUE #> 3   111111118 1911-11-01 114 kvinde 1118 FALSE  TRUE  # Custom column names: cpr_info(df, pnr, add = c(birth_date = "bday", years_old = "age")) #>           pnr birth_date years_old #> 1  1111111118 1911-11-11       114 #> 2 111111-1118 1911-11-11       114 #> 3   111111118 1911-11-01       114  # Unnamed — uses type names directly: cpr_info(df, pnr, add = c("bday", "sex")) #>           pnr       bday    sex #> 1  1111111118 1911-11-11 kvinde #> 2 111111-1118 1911-11-11 kvinde #> 3   111111118 1911-11-01 kvinde

title: Assert that a data frame is empty — expect_empty

title: Assert that a data frame is empty — expect_empty
code: expect_empty(   data,   success_msg = "The dataset is empty.",   warn_msg = "The dataset is not empty.",   abort_msg = NULL,   log = NULL )

title: Assert that a data frame is empty — expect_empty
text: data data frame tibble. success_msg Message shown (logged) data empty. Default: "dataset empty.". warn_msg Message shown (logged) data empty abort_msg set. Default: "dataset empty.". abort_msg provided, cli::cli_abort() called message data empty. NULL (default), warning issued instead. log Optional path log file. directory created automatically exist. entry prefixed timestamp symbol (✔, ✖, !).

title: Assert that a data frame is empty — expect_empty
text: data invisibly.

title: Assert that a data frame is empty — expect_empty
code: # Success — no rows data.frame() |> expect_empty() #> ✔ The dataset is empty.  # Warning — unexpected rows dplyr::filter(ggplot2::mpg, cyl < 0) |>   expect_empty(warn_msg = "Negative cylinder counts found") #> ✔ The dataset is empty.  # Abort — treat unexpected rows as a hard error if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ dplyr::filter(dplyr::starwars, height < 0) |>   expect_empty(abort_msg = "Impossible: negative height") } # }  # With logging: if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ log_path <- f("log/{nowf()}/checks.log") checker  <- purrr::partial(expect_empty, log = log_path)  dplyr::filter(dplyr::starwars, name == "Harry Potter") |>   checker(success_msg = "No Harry Potter rows") } # }

code: f(   ...,   .sep = "",   .envir = parent.frame(),   .open = "{",   .close = "}",   .na = "NA",   .null = character(),   .comment = "#",   .literal = FALSE,   .transformer = identity_transformer,   .trim = TRUE )

title: String interpolation shorthand — f
text: ... [expressions] Unnamed arguments taken expression string(s) format. Multiple inputs concatenated together formatting. Named arguments taken temporary variables available substitution. glue_data(), elements ... override values .x. .sep [character(1): ‘""’] Separator used separate elements. .envir [environment: parent.frame()] Environment evaluate expression . Expressions evaluated left right. .x environment, expressions evaluated environment .envir ignored. NULL passed, equivalent emptyenv(). .open [character(1): ‘\{’] opening delimiter. Doubling full delimiter escapes . .close [character(1): ‘\}’] closing delimiter. Doubling full delimiter escapes . .na [character(1): ‘NA’] Value replace NA values . NULL missing values propagated, NA result cause NA output. Otherwise value replaced value .na. .null [character(1): ‘character()’] Value replace NULL values . character() whole output character(). NULL NULL values dropped (paste0()). Otherwise value replaced value .null. .comment [character(1): ‘#’] Value use comment character. .literal [boolean(1): ‘FALSE’] Whether treat single double quotes, backticks, comments regular characters (vs. syntactic elements), parsing expression string. Setting .literal = TRUE probably makes sense combination custom .transformer, case glue_col(). Regard argument (especially, name) experimental. .transformer [function] function taking two arguments, text envir, text unparsed string inside glue block envir execution environment. .transformer lets modify glue block , , evaluation, allowing create custom glue()-like functions. See vignette("transformers") examples. .trim [logical(1): ‘TRUE’] Whether trim input template trim() .

title: Find sign combinations that sum to a target — find_signs
text: df data frame containing labels values. label_col Column containing item labels (unquoted). value_col Column containing numeric values (unquoted). total_label Label row whose value reconciliation target. Default: "total". positive Character vector labels must receive positive sign. Optional. negative Character vector labels must receive negative sign. Optional. Optional grouping columns (unquoted). supplied, reconciliation performed separately group. max_zeros Maximum number zero coefficients allowed valid solution (.e. many items can excluded). Default: 2L.

title: Find sign combinations that sum to a target — find_signs
text: tibble label/value structure input, values multiplied resolved signs. Groups unique solution silently dropped (NULL).

title: Read one or more files with automatic format detection — read_files

text: paths single file path character vector file paths. vector unnamed, file names (without extension) used list names. ... Additional arguments forwarded underlying reader.

title: Read one or more files with automatic format detection — read_files
text: single path: object returned reader. multiple paths: named list objects.

title: Read one or more files with automatic format detection — read_files
text: Supported formats: Note CSV: read_files() uses readr::read_csv2() .csv files, expects semicolon-separated values comma decimal mark (Danish/European convention). CSV uses commas separators, pass file directly readr::read_csv() instead. Packages non-base formats (arrow, haven, readxl, jsonlite, yaml) must installed separately.

title: Read one or more files with automatic format detection — read_files
code: if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ # Single file df <- read_files("data/results.parquet")  # Multiple files — returns a named list files <- read_files(c("data/a.csv", "data/b.csv"))  # Pipeline with require_files() and bind_files(): require_files("data/dat{0:9}.parquet") |>   read_files() |>   bind_files() } # }

text: named character vector validated file paths.

title: Validate and resolve file paths — require_files

what: Retrieve objects matching a pattern from an environment — summon
title: Retrieve objects matching a pattern from an environment — summon
text: Searches environment objects whose names match regular expression returns named list. Useful collecting family similarly-named objects (e.g. dat0 dat9) unpacking list unpack_files().

title: Retrieve objects matching a pattern from an environment — summon
code: summon(pattern, .envir = parent.frame())

title: Retrieve objects matching a pattern from an environment — summon
text: pattern single regular expression string. .envir environment search. Default calling environment.

title: Retrieve objects matching a pattern from an environment — summon
text: named list matching objects.

title: Retrieve objects matching a pattern from an environment — summon
code: dat1 <- data.frame(x = 1) dat2 <- data.frame(x = 2) dat3 <- data.frame(x = 3) summon("^dat\\d+$") #> $dat1 #>   x #> 1 1 #>  #> $dat2 #>   x #> 1 2 #>  #> $dat3 #>   x #> 1 3 #>

title: Interactive time-series screening dashboard — time2screen
text: Launches Shiny application visually reviewing time-series data group group. columns x, y, series, excluded become grouping dimensions dropdown selectors. Navigate groups arrow keys ← / → buttons. Press Esc exit.

title: Interactive time-series screening dashboard — time2screen
code: time2screen(data, x, y, series = NULL, .exclude = NULL, .from_zero = FALSE)

title: Interactive time-series screening dashboard — time2screen
text: data data frame containing minimum x-axis column, y-axis column, least one grouping column. x Time (x-axis) variable. Accepts Date, POSIXct, numeric years, value coercible date. (Unquoted.) y Numeric measurement (y-axis) variable. (Unquoted.) series Optional variable plotting multiple lines per group (e.g. category). (Unquoted.) .exclude Columns exclude becoming grouping dropdowns. (Unquoted, tidy-select.) .from_zero TRUE, y-axis starts zero. Can also toggled interactively via "Start zero" button. Default: FALSE.

title: Interactive time-series screening dashboard — time2screen
text: Shiny app object. interactive session app displayed immediately; otherwise launched browser.

title: Interactive time-series screening dashboard — time2screen
text: Requires shiny highcharter packages.

title: Interactive time-series screening dashboard — time2screen
code: if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ library(dplyr)  # Simple example with economics dataset: ggplot2::economics_long |>   time2screen(date, value, series = variable)  # With a grouping column: df <- data.frame(   year    = rep(2010:2020, 3),   country = rep(c("DK", "SE", "NO"), each = 11),   gdp     = rnorm(33, 300, 20) ) time2screen(df, x = year, y = gdp) } # }

what: Track the last data frame printed in the console — track_last_df
title: Track the last data frame printed in the console — track_last_df
text: Installs task callback automatically saves data frame returned console named variable global environment. Intermediate expressions (1 + 1, plots, etc.) ignored — top-level data frame returns captured.

title: Track the last data frame printed in the console — track_last_df
code: track_last_df(on = TRUE, name = ".last.df")

title: Track the last data frame printed in the console — track_last_df
text: TRUE enable tracking (default), FALSE disable. name Name variable written .GlobalEnv. Default: ".last.df".

title: Track the last data frame printed in the console — track_last_df
text: TRUE invisibly.

title: Track the last data frame printed in the console — track_last_df
text: Calling track_last_df() replaces existing callback, preventing duplicates.

title: Track the last data frame printed in the console — track_last_df
code: if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ track_last_df()          # enable dplyr::starwars |> head() .last.df                 # the last printed data frame  track_last_df(FALSE)     # disable } # }

what: Unpack a named list into individual variables — unpack_files
title: Unpack a named list into individual variables — unpack_files
text: Assigns element named list variable target environment. complement read_files() want individual named objects rather single list.

title: Unpack a named list into individual variables — unpack_files
code: unpack_files(data, .envir = parent.frame(), .overwrite = FALSE)

title: Unpack a named list into individual variables — unpack_files
text: data fully named list. .envir Target environment. Default calling environment. .overwrite FALSE (default), aborts name already exists target environment. Set TRUE allow overwrites.

title: Unpack a named list into individual variables — unpack_files
text: data invisibly.

title: Unpack a named list into individual variables — unpack_files
code: if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ # Read files into separate variables: require_files("data/dat{0:9}.parquet") |>   read_files() |>   unpack_files()  # dat0, dat1, ..., dat9 now exist in the environment. # Collect them again: summon("^dat\\d+$") } # }

what: Compare column types across data frames — view_types
title: Compare column types across data frames — view_types
text: Shows column types one data frames side--side. Useful diagnosing type mismatches join call bind_files().

title: Compare column types across data frames — view_types
code: view_types(..., diff = FALSE, focus = NULL)

title: Compare column types across data frames — view_types
text: ... Data frames compare (unquoted). Names inferred expressions passed. diff Logical. TRUE, columns types differ across datasets shown. Default: FALSE. focus named length-1 character vector, e.g. c(year = "int"). supplied, named column shown, datasets type match expected type. Returns empty tibble (0 rows) types match — suitable use tests.

title: Compare column types across data frames — view_types
text: tibble column column one column per input dataset showing pillar::type_sum() type string.

title: Compare column types across data frames — view_types
code: df1 <- data.frame(x = 1L, y = "a") df2 <- data.frame(x = 1.0, y = "b")  view_types(df1, df2) #> # A tibble: 2 × 3 #>   column df1   df2   #>   <chr>  <chr> <chr> #> 1 x      int   dbl   #> 2 y      chr   chr   view_types(df1, df2, diff = TRUE) #> # A tibble: 1 × 3 #>   column df1   df2   #>   <chr>  <chr> <chr> #> 1 x      int   dbl   view_types(df1, df2, focus = c(x = "int")) #> # A tibble: 1 × 2 #>   column df2   #>   <chr>  <chr> #> 1 x      dbl
